text: The patient is positioned in the Trendelenburg position with the left side elevated, preparing for laparoscopic anterior resection.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, located just below the duodenal jugular flexure.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to clear nodal tissues at the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, located just below the duodenal jugular flexure.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, taking precautions to avoid injuring the surrounding plexus.
The surgeon continues dissection along the inferior mesenteric artery, ensuring precision to avoid damaging surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the inferior mesenteric artery, taking precautions to avoid injuring the left ureter, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects just behind the inferior mesenteric artery, ensuring the surrounding neural plexus remains intact.
The camera trocar is positioned slightly to the right of the umbilicus, enhancing the visual exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon visually identifies the inferior mesenteric artery during the dissection, ensuring it remains within the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds with dissection from a medial to lateral direction, carefully navigating around the inferior mesenteric artery while maintaining awareness of the left ureter to avoid injury.
The surgeon begins dissection at the level of the sacral promontory, where the anatomical planes are clearly identifiable, facilitating the separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon enters the proper dissection plane behind the inferior mesenteric artery, with clear visualization aided by the camera trocar placement slightly to the right of the umbilicus.
The surgeon dissects anterior to Toldt's fascia, navigating the anatomical plane in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon continues dissection within the identified plane, minimizing the risk of injuring the gonadal vessels and ureter.
The surgeon identifies the blue gonadal vessel and the ureter, which is situated in a slightly deeper plane within the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds with cranial dissection, staying superficial and anterior to the serotus fascia, ensuring the dissection remains in the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon returns to the dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery, carefully navigating the anatomical plane to avoid injury to surrounding structures.
The surgeon clears nodal tissues, maintaining dissection in the plane anterior to the Tol's fascia, with visible gonadal vessels and ureter in deeper planes.
The surgeon prepares to divide the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin, a crucial step in ultra low anterior resection.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) just distal to the origin of the left colic artery, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin, providing additional length to the vascular pedicle, facilitating the colon's reach to the pelvic floor.
The surgeon uses a clip, vessel sealing device, and harmonic scalpel to divide the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), with visible precision and control over the vascular structure.
The surgeon performs dissection anterior to the left kidney, carefully separating tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon identifies the proper dissection plane and uses diathermy or a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect the tissue, minimizing bleeding and maintaining a clean surgical field.
The surgical team proceeds towards the pelvis, continuing the dissection and division of structures in the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field at the level of the pelvis, taking precautions to avoid injury to the ureter and hypogastric nerves, which are visible in close proximity to the dissection area.
The surgeon identifies the sacral promontory, where the hypogastric nerves are visible running on either side, ensuring careful dissection to avoid nerve injury.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection near the periprostatic neural plexus, carefully navigating around the visible nerve structures.
The surgeon completes the medial to lateral dissection and proceeds to divide the lateral attachments of the colon, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon extends the dissection to mobilize the splenic flexure, facilitating its separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the left colic artery, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric vein, preparing to ligate it near the duodenal jejunal flexure.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon clips and divides the lateral attachment of the colon following the medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon prepares to take the inferior mesenteric artery and vein, along with their draining lymph nodes, to the specimen side, following their dissection and ligation.
The surgeon prepares the rectum for stapler application, ensuring proper alignment and exposure of the rectal tissue for the upcoming stapling procedure.
The surgeon divides the mesorectum perpendicular to the rectum, approximately five centimeters distal to the tumor, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon displays the rectum, which has been carefully prepared for stapler application, showing clear demarcation and readiness for the next surgical step.
The surgeon uses a blue reload stapler to divide the rectum, ensuring precise separation of the tissue.
The surgical field shows the rectum prepared for stapler application with no significant bleeding visible.
The surgeon uses a stapler to completely divide the rectum with a single fire, ensuring a clean separation without contamination.
The surgeon introduces an EEA stapler through the anus, with the trocar piercing the rectal stump.
An instrument is positioned to support the rectal stump, stabilizing it during the procedure.
The surgeon attaches the anvil to the head of the EEA stapler, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
The EEA stapler, introduced through the anus, is fired from below to create an anastomosis, with the assembly of the stapler visibly completed.
Following the anastomosis, the surgeon checks for leaks by instilling saline into the pelvis and inflating air through the anus, observing for any bubbles indicating a leak.
The surgeon places a drainage tube in the surgical site and carefully extracts the resected specimen, ensuring adequate wound protection during the process.
